original_abstract: Text classification serves as a cornerstone for numerous natural language processing applications. While traditional classifiers often depend on labor-intensive, handcrafted features—such as specialized dictionaries, external knowledge bases, and complex tree kernels—this study proposes a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) that learns directly from raw data. Our architecture utilizes a recurrent structure to capture broad contextual information during word representation learning, effectively reducing the noise typically introduced by standard window-based neural networks. Furthermore, we integrate a max-pooling layer to automatically identify and prioritize the most influential words within a text. Evaluating our model across four benchmark datasets, we demonstrate that this approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods, showing particularly significant improvements in document-level classification tasks.
humanized_abstract: Text classification is a fundamental task in natural language processing. Many conventional approaches rely on manually engineered features such as domain dictionaries, lexical resources, or tree kernel-based features, which are often time-consuming to prepare. In this paper, we propose a new method called the Recurrent Convolutional Neural Network (RCNN) that learns the features from scratch, without needing to engineer any additional knowledge. The RCNN learns word representations that capture global information, like the wording pattern in a document, while excluding the noise that is introduced by the local neighborhood description and that are modeled by conventional window-based neural networks. To select the most important words in a text that contribute the most to the classification, we employ a max-pooling layer. Experimental results on four datasets have demonstrated that our approach not only achieves state-of-the-art performance but also shows significant improvement when classifying a full document.
